checks.a_analysis: The output JSON contains the document title, update date, and 171 specific board/agency headings, all of which are present in the source PDF. No fabricated body text or invented sections were found. The structure accurately reflects the contents of the Rulemaking Coordinators list.

A thorough audit of random and specific contact entries (e.g., 911 Board, Appraisal Board, Dental Examiners, Veterinary Medical Board) was performed. All phone numbers, email addresses, and mailing addresses in the JSON perfectly match the source PDF. For instance, the phone number for the Engineers and Surveyors Board (919.791.2000 ext. 111) and the email for the Child Care Commission (dedra.alston@dhhs.nc.gov) are correctly converted.

The source document contains 10 pages. The JSON conversion includes content from every page, starting with the 911 Board on page 1 and ending with the Wildlife Resources Commission on page 10. Every intermediate section checked (e.g., Battleship Commission on p1, Crime Victims on p2, Foresters on p3, etc.) is present in the output.
The document consists of a multi-column list of contact blocks rather than formal tables. As there are no data tables to evaluate for row counts, this check is considered passed by default.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: Each section/heading was compared for content completeness. For example, the list of NCAC chapters for the Department of Public Safety (14B NCAC 01 through 18) and the Social Services Commission (10A NCAC 6 through 97) are complete in the JSON. No material text content within sections was found to be missing.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The wording in the JSON preserves the meaning and technical data of the source. Minor formatting adjustments (like line breaks within address strings) are acceptable. Spelling of names, board titles, and regulatory citations (NCAC numbers) are accurate.

checks.a_analysis: I scanned the JSON output for any repeated sections or paragraphs that only appear once in the source PDF. The document consists of a long list of contact entries, and while some individuals (e.g., Anna Hayworth) are listed under multiple different boards as rulemaking coordinators, this accurately reflects the source document. No unintended duplications of content blocks were found.

The main document title 'Rulemaking Coordinators' is correctly identified at the top level (as the section title). All subsequent agency and board names (e.g., '911 Board', 'Acupuncture Licensing Board') are assigned as Heading Level 2 (H2). This creates a consistent and flat hierarchy that correctly reflects the structure of the directory. There are no skipped levels (e.g., H1 to H3).

The source document uses a multi-column layout to display contact information blocks, but does not use formal grid-based tables with defined headers. The conversion has correctly interpreted these as a series of headings followed by paragraphs, which preserves the structural meaning of the information better than attempting to force them into a table structure. Thus, no corrupted tables exist.

The source PDF is organized in a three-column layout. I verified the reading order of the entries on several pages (including pages 1, 3, and 10). The JSON output correctly follows the column-wise reading order (Column 1 top-to-bottom, then Column 2, then Column 3) and maintains the logical sequence across page boundaries. For example, the transition from 'Battleship Commission' at the end of page 1 to 'Blind, Commission for the' at the start of page 2 is correctly ordered in the JSON.

The contact information blocks in the source are essentially lists of data (Rule, Name, Address, Phone, Email), but they are not formatted as bulleted or numbered lists in the source PDF. The conversion has rendered these as paragraphs with newline characters, which preserves the intended grouping and sequence of information. No actual lists from the source were misidentified or corrupted.

The document contains numerous email addresses. I verified a representative sample across multiple pages (e.g., leigh.jackson@nc.gov on page 1, info@ncbrf.org on page 3, and rulemaking@ncdot.gov on page 10). The JSON output correctly identifies these as 'mailto:' links. No fabricated links were added, and no visible links in the source were missed.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF uses a multi-column layout to list various agencies and their rulemaking coordinators. The HTML output converts this into a single-column layout with a persistent sidebar table of contents for navigation. This is a standard and expected transformation for responsive web content and does not compromise the visual organization or flow of information. The order of the agencies matches the source material.
The source PDF does not contain any formal data tables with rows and columns; it consists of text blocks for contact information. Therefore, no table rendering issues are present.
The source PDF is a text-only document containing no meaningful images, logos, or charts. No images were expected in the output.

A review of the HTML rendering shows no instances of duplicated body content. While agency names appear in both the sidebar navigation and the main body, this is an intentional accessibility feature for navigation (WCAG). Each contact block appears only once in the body content corresponding to its location in the PDF.
